retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid region has an extensive blood supply. The main arterial supply to the thyroid gland is the superior and inferior thyroid arteries. These two arteries have a rich collateral system composed of many branches and anastomoses, both ipsilaterally and contralaterally. The superior thyroid artery is a branch of the external carotid artery. It courses under the omohyoid and sternohyoid muscles and runs lateral to the larynx. The cricothyroid artery, a branch of the superior thyroid artery, enters the cricothyroid muscle’s superficial surface and contributes to its blood supply. The inferior thyroid artery is a branch of the thyrocervical trunk, which arises from the subclavian artery. Most of its branches supply blood to the lateral aspect of the thyroid gland and the lateral muscles of the thyroid region. Specifically, the muscular branches of the inferior lateral artery supply the infrahyoid, anterior scalene, longus colli, and inferior pharyngeal constrictor muscles. In 10% of the population, an additional artery called the thyroid ima may be present and contribute to the blood supply of the thyroid.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Posterior Auricular Artery -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior auricular artery's perfusion territory includes four main muscles: The posterior auricular muscles The occipitalis muscle The temporalis muscle The sternocleidomastoid muscle (origin) These muscles will also derive blood from other arteries, but some of their muscle fibers receive some blood from the perfusion territory of the posterior auricular artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: While inferior to the hyoid bone, there is another group of four paired muscles called the infrahyoid muscles: sternothyroid, sternohyoid, thyrohyoid, and omohyoid. The major function of these muscles depresses the hyoid bone and elevates the larynx to facilitate swallowing. While all the infrahyoid muscles depress the hyoid bone, the sternohyoid muscles also depress the larynx due to their shared origin from the anterior surface of the sternoclavicular joint to attach to the body of the hyoid bone. This is in contrast with the thyrohyoid muscle that elevates the larynx because it starts at the thyroid cartilage and attaches to the greater horn of the hyoid bone. The omohyoid muscle is unique because it has two bellies similar to the digastric muscle. This muscle starts from the superior border of the scapula and attaches to the body of the hyoid bone. Along with the attachment to the hyoid bone, the omohyoid muscle also serves a minor role in the protection of the carotid sheath's contents.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The suprahyoid artery supplies the omohyoid, sternothyroid, and thyrohyoid muscles. They anastomose with the corresponding vessels from the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Suprahyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: All suprahyoid muscles receive vascular supply from branches of the external carotid artery. The anterior belly of the digastric muscle is supplied by the submental branch of the facial artery, while the posterior belly receives blood from the occipital and posterior auricular arteries. Branches of the lingual artery supply the geniohyoid muscle. The mylohyoid muscle receives blood from the submental branch of the facial artery and the mylohyoid branch of the inferior alveolar artery. [18] Lymph from the suprahyoid region flows into the submental and submandibular lymph nodes before ultimately draining into the deep cervical lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Vascular Supply and Drainage
retrieved_docs.content: The superior thyroid vein originates at the upper pole of the thyroid gland from the thyroid venous plexus. Traveling superiorly and laterally, it crosses over the superior belly of the omohyoid muscle and the common carotid artery before draining into the internal jugular vein. The superior thyroid artery accompanies this vein, which primarily drains venous blood from the thyroid gland and larynx, receiving tributaries from the superior laryngeal and cricothyroid veins. The middle thyroid vein is the shortest of the thyroid veins. This vein originates on the lateral surface of the thyroid lobe at the midthyroid level and then crosses the common carotid artery to empty directly into the internal jugular vein. The inferior thyroid vein arises from the lower border of the thyroid isthmus and typically appears as paired veins on the right and left. These veins descend to form the pretracheal venous plexus, which lies anterior to the trachea and deep into the sternothyroid muscle. The inferior thyroid veins are the largest and most variable among the thyroid veins, often showing asymmetry and differing patterns in number, course, and termination points. [7] See Image. Arterial and Venous Anatomy of the Thyroid Gland.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Stylohyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The stylohyoid muscle receives its vascular supply from branches of the external carotid artery, and the occipital branch of the external carotid artery gives branches to the stylohyoid. Lymphatic drainage from this part of the neck goes into the submental and submandibular group of lymph nodes, and then it drains into the deep cervical group of lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The mylohyoid muscle mainly obtains its blood supply from the mylohyoid artery, a branch of the inferior alveolar artery, which is a branch of the first portion of the maxillary artery. The maxillary artery supplies components within the face and is a tributary of the external carotid artery formed deep to the mandible neck. A common course of the maxillary artery is from its origin point deep to the mandible neck extending between the mandible ramus and ligament of the sphenomandibular to the lateral pterygoid muscle and finally to the fossa of the pterygopalatine. Hence, one frequently sees references to the maxillary artery as having three divisions, which include the pterygoid, pterygopalatine, and mandibular segments. The mandibular portion (bony portion) has five major branches including the middle meningeal artery, accessory meningeal artery, deep auricular artery, anterior tympanic artery, and the inferior alveolar artery. The inferior alveolar artery yields the mylohyoid branch just before entering the mandibular foramen. The mylohyoid branch runs in the mylohyoid groove where it functions as the primary vascular source for the mylohyoid muscle. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Anterior Triangle
retrieved_docs.content: There are eight branches of the external carotid artery, though only the four most proximal are within the boundaries of the anterior triangle. The superior thyroid artery exits from the anterior aspect of the carotid artery and descends to supply the superior pole of the thyroid gland, the thyrohyoid and cricothyroid muscles, and the internal larynx via the superior laryngeal artery. The ascending pharyngeal artery arises from the posterior aspect of the external carotid, just superior to the superior thyroid artery. This artery dives deep to pierce the pretracheal fascia and supply pharyngeal constrictor muscles. The lingual artery originates from the anterior surface of the external carotid and immediately passes deep to the mylohyoid muscle to supply the muscles of the tongue and the sublingual salivary gland. The facial artery also branches off of the anterior surface of the external carotid just superior to the lingual artery. It then passes deep to the posterior belly of the digastric and stylohyoid muscles and continues its tortuous course superficial to the mylohyoid muscle before hooking over the inferior edge of the mandible just anterior to the masseter muscle. The facial artery supplies the submandibular salivary gland, the soft palate, and many more structures of the face via its numerous branches. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Arytenoid Cartilage -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Blood supply to the arytenoid, as well as the entire larynx, is derived from the superior and inferior thyroid arteries. The superior thyroid artery branches from the external carotid artery and gives the superior laryngeal artery. The superior laryngeal artery accompanies the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve through the thyrohyoid membrane to supply the supraglottic region. It ends in the anastomotic network in front of the cricothyroid membrane. The inferior thyroid artery comes off the thyrocervical trunk, itself originating from the subclavian artery and gives a terminal small caliber artery that accompanies the terminal segment of the recurrent laryngeal nerve. This vessel supplies the infraglottic larynx and gives two terminal small artery branches. The first branch supplies the posterior cricoarytenoid muscle, and the second passes along the lower border of the cricoid cartilage. Both of the two terminal branches give many anastomotic arteries to the terminals of the superior laryngeal artery. The drainage of the supraglottic structures (epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids, and false cords) follows the superior laryngeal and superior thyroid vessels. Thus, the lymphatics flow from the piriform sinus through the thyrohyoid membrane to end primarily in the deep jugular chain around the carotid bifurcation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The external carotid arteries have eight major divisions that supply the head, neck, and face. As the external carotid arteries ascend the neck, it gives off a branch called the superior thyroid artery. The superior thyroid artery is vital for the muscles, organs, and structures predominantly inferior to the hyoid bone like the thyroid gland, infrahyoid muscles, and sternocleidomastoid muscle, but also the larynx above the hyoid bone. The second branch from the external carotid arteries is the ascending pharyngeal arteries. These arteries supply the middle ear, pharynx part of the oral cavity, prevertebral muscles and ascend to supply the meninges. The next branch that arises is the lingual artery that supplies the floor of the mouth and tongue. As the external carotid artery approach the mandible, it gives off the facial artery. The facial artery has multiple regions of the face, palate part of the oral cavity and tonsils, and submandibular glands that it supplies. The external carotid supplies the scalp through the next branch, which is the occipital artery. The posterior auricular artery is the last branch before the external carotid artery terminates into end branches. This artery is important in supplying the ear, scalp, cranial never VII, and parts of the scalp. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Costocervical Trunk Arteries -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Although the costocervical trunk commonly originates from the second part of the subclavian artery, it can originate from its third part or be absent en bloc. The two usual branches of the costocervical trunk include the deep cervical artery and the superior intercostal artery, which can arise independently from the subclavian artery if the trunk is absent. The superior intercostal artery can originate from the thyrocervical trunk or some of its branches like the inferior thyroid artery and the scapular artery. There are also reports that the superior intercostal artery can originate from the axillary artery, aortic arch, vertebral artery, or the second intercostal artery [21] [22] [23] [24] [25] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Costocervical Trunk Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The costocervical trunk is a branch of the subclavian artery which supplies the upper extremities and contributes partly to the blood supply of the head and neck. The subclavian artery has a different origin on each side. On the right side, it originates from the brachiocephalic trunk while stemming directly from the arch of the aorta on the left side. [1] [2] It is critical to know the course of the subclavian artery, its branches, and its parts to get a comprehensive understanding of the costocervical trunk. The subclavian artery divides into three segments depending on their position relative to the scalenus anterior muscle. The first part of the subclavian artery extends from its root to the medial part of the scalenus muscle. The first segment of the subclavian artery gives rise to the internal thoracic artery(internal mammary artery) [3] , vertebral artery, and thyrocervical trunk that supply the circle of Willis, breast tissue and thyroid tissue. The second part of the subclavian artery starts from the medial part of the scalenus muscle and extends to the lateral aspect of the scalenus muscle; This part gives rise to the costocervical trunk. The third part starts at the lateral aspect of the scalenus muscle and ends at the tip of the first rib, where it becomes the axillary artery. [4] [5] Since we now know the exact origin of the costocervical trunk, we can go in-depth about delineating its details and importance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Arteries -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the vascular supply of the carotid arteries is crucial clinically. The muscles of mastication receive vascular supply from the maxillary artery and facial artery, branches of the ECA. The facial artery also supplies muscles of facial expression. The occipital artery, another branch of the ECA, supplies blood to the sternocleidomastoid, trapezius, and deep muscles of the back. The superficial temporal artery supplies the temporal muscle. The extraocular muscles get their vascular supply by branches of the ophthalmic artery, which is a branch of the ICA. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Suprahyoid Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Eagle syndrome is more frequently observed in individuals with Turner syndrome than in the general population. [34] In the vascular variant of the syndrome, compression of the internal carotid artery may occur. In some cases, compression of the internal jugular vein may also be observed. [35] Surgical resection of the structure causing compression, whether the ossified ligament or the elongated styloid process, is the primary treatment approach. [36] In some animal species, the stylohyoid ligament undergoes complete ossification, forming a distinct epihyal bone.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sternohyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The external carotid artery is the vessel that perfuses the structures in the face and neck. The external carotid artery adequately perfumes the head, face, and neck through several arterial branches. One of the branches formed is the superior thyroid artery. After the superior thyroid artery branches from the external carotid artery. It descends towards the thyroid gland. As the superior thyroid artery makes its way to the thyroid gland, it will perfuse the structures with which it comes in contact. One of the structures that the superior thyroid artery passes is the sternohyoid muscle. The sternohyoid muscle will receive its arterial blood supply from the superior thyroid artery. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: and peripheral/visceral atherosclerosis, all of which contribute to the risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, illustrating the systemic impact of vascular disease on cerebral blood flow. These entities are organized hierarchically: general cardiac and vascular diagnoses and risk factors feed into specific diagnostic categories; diagnostic categories give rise to biopsy techniques; biopsy techniques serve as parents for detailed ventricular or pericardial biopsy procedures; biopsies and excisions connect to broader operative groups such as “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” and “other or heart procedures.” This integrated framework delineates the relationships among anatomical structures (heart, pericardium, papillary muscles), diagnostic modalities, therapeutic interventions, and related disease conditions, supporting comprehensive cardiac and vascular patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: situating basilar‑artery embolism as a type of cerebral‑artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي) within the broader cerebrovascular disease framework. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how occlusive disease of precerebral and coronary arteries produces infarction in the brain and heart, how the basilar artery serves as a central anatomical locus, and how imaging findings, morphological patterns, and surgical interventions are integrated into patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: الشريانية (SMQ)), linking cardiac infarction to the same thrombo‑embolic mechanisms that produce cerebral infarcts. Collectively, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, basilar artery stenosis, thrombosis, and embolism, as well as non‑Q wave and subendocardial myocardial infarctions, represent a unified vascular pathology characterized by atherosclerotic change, thrombo‑embolic obstruction, and the common morphological outcome of infarction (الاحتشاء). These entities are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (intracranial arteries, anterolateral myocardium), common procedural interventions (arterial endarterectomy, endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels), and overlapping clinical classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of thrombo‑embolic disease in both the cerebral and cardiac circulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: specific precerebral arterial lesions basilar artery stenosis (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي) and basilar artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي) are subtypes of general arterial stenosis (تضيق الشرايين) and arterial occlusion (انسداد الشرايين). Basilar artery embolism (embolie; arteria basilaris) is mapped to this category and is defined as an inverse_isa of انصمام الشريان المخي, emphasizing its role as a distinct embolic event within the same pathophysiological framework. The anatomical substrate for these processes is the Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as the site of atherosclerotic change and the indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a specialized form of arterial endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). The SMQ grouping حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases) is a member of both the precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis spectrum and the endarterectomy procedural domain, underscoring the therapeutic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 169

Parallel mechanisms operate in the heart. Non‑Q wave myocardial infarction and its child Acute non‑Q wave infarction – anterolateral (an inverse_isa relationship) share the same infarction morphology (الاحتشاء) and are localized to the Structure of myocardium of anterolateral region, a substructure of the Myocardium and Cardiac Conduction System. Subendocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف) likewise carries the الاحتشاء descriptor. Acute myocardial infarction includes arterial thrombosis and embolism (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ)), linking cardiac infarction to the same thrombo‑embolic mechanisms that produce cerebral infarcts. Collectively, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, basilar artery

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function includes several distinct conditions that are important for obstetric care: الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), رحم ناقص التوتر (uterus with insufficient tone), and عطالة رحمية ثانوية (secondary uterine inertia). These entities represent specific abnormalities of the uterus and placenta that can affect labor progression and fetal outcomes, and they are considered sub‑categories of overall uterine function in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: situates these processes in the basilar artery (Arteria basilaris) and the broader Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as a finding site for occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracranial atherosclerosis, and as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of head and neck vessels (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) under the broader procedure category endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarctions (احتشاء دماغي) result from embolism, thrombosis, or occlusion of the precerebral arteries—including the basilar artery—or from basilar‑artery stenosis (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي). These events are classified under the morphological term الاحتشاء (infarction) and are directly linked to vascular occlusion (انسداد الأوعية الدموية), a manifestation of an unspecified circulatory system disorder (اضطراب في جهاز الدورة الدموية غير محدد) that also encompasses chronic rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي مزمن). Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a central arterial occlusion entity (inverse_isa انسداد الشرايين) and includes the clinical group of arterial thrombosis and embolism cases (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية SMQ). It is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) and may be treated by endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان). Basilar‑artery embolism (embolie; arteria basilaris) is the inverse of cerebral artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي) and represents a specific form of arterial embolic disease. Basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي) and basilar‑artery stenosis are mapped to the broader category of arterial stenosis (تضيق الشرايين). The same term الاحتشاء also describes myocardial infarction of the heart muscle beneath the diaphragm (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف), highlighting the shared pathology of tissue necrosis across organ systems. Together, these relationships illustrate the interconnected nature of precerebral arterial occlusive disease, its clinical manifestations in the brain and heart, and the diagnostic and surgical interventions such as endarterectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 222
retrieved_docs.content: interventions for arterial diseases excluding the aorta (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالشرايين (باستثناء الأورطي)) comprise the same surgical and endovascular procedures—excision of the carotid artery interior, aneurysm excision, percutaneous coronary intervention and coronary artery grafting—providing clinicians with a classification framework that integrates vascular treatment choices with positional factors affecting blood‑pressure regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction caused by embolism of the precerebral arteries, by thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, or by occlusion of the basilar artery is described with the morphological hallmark الاحتشاء (infarction), a finding that is also linked to the cardiac entity احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف (heart‑muscle infarction). الاحتشاء is in turn related to انسداد الأوعية الدموية (vascular occlusion), emphasizing that tissue infarction typically follows an occlusive vascular event. Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion is mapped to occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, which is identified as the upstream source of both embolic and thrombotic precerebral arterial infarcts and as the direct cause of basilar‑artery infarction; thus, occlusion/stenosis of precerebral arteries serves as a central mechanistic node linking these stroke entities. Within the broader nosology, the category Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System is a child of transient cerebral

retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall intracranial arterial architecture. This anatomical framework also serves as a common site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting the importance of evaluating the basilar artery and related intracranial vessels for sclerotic changes that can impact cerebral blood flow and patient outcomes.

Dyspareunia of non‑psychogenic origin is recognized as a condition of the female genital organs and is situated within the broader spectrum of female genital and obstetric procedures. A separate vascular sub‑network links the aorta to major surgeries: the aorta is the direct_procedure_site_of aortic resection, replacement, and anastomosis, and the same relationship connects the aorta to fetal monitoring in the context of female genital organs. The qualifier Allowed is used to denote abnormalities of the amnion and of the os pubis, as well as anatomical‑histological aspects of the aorta (innervation, growth and development). Overall, the integrated framework connects maternal‑fetal care, cervical pathology, delivery assistance, device‑based interventions, abortive and ectopic‑pregnancy management, imaging and monitoring, and related vascular procedures, illustrating how each entity and relationship contributes to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: The left coronary artery branch věnčité tepny is a common anatomical site for coronary artery occlusion, embolism, spasm, atherosclerotic change and thrombosis. The anterolateral region of the myocardium is the typical location of acute myocardial infarction, which is classified both as a specific cardiac muscle formation process and as a sub‑category of arterial thrombosis/embolism, highlighting their shared pathophysiology. Intracranial arteries serve as a finding site for intracranial atherosclerosis and as an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels. Endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels is a subtype of arterial endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان) and is encompassed within the broader arterial thrombosis and embolism framework. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate the hierarchical, locational, pathological and temporal relationships among atherosclerotic disease, inflammatory vascular changes, acute coronary syndromes, their
